summary: Graham argues that programming languages are not equivalent, and that more powerful languages like Lisp give significant leverage on hard problems, especially in competitive software businesses. He criticizes managerial preference for “standard” technologies as a risk-avoidance habit that leads to average outcomes, and claims that choosing powerful tools can be a decisive advantage.
key_points: The belief that all languages are equivalent is convenient but false; languages differ in expressive power and suitability.
Lisp’s design came from mathematical ideas, which explains why its core power remains relevant decades later.
Powerful languages enable shorter, more adaptable programs via bottom-up programming and macros.
Concerns about hiring and compatibility are often overstated compared to the competitive advantages of leverage.
Choosing “industry best practice” tends to produce average results and slower development.
money_quotes: The disadvantage of believing that all programming languages are equivalent is that it's not true. But the advantage is that it makes your life a lot simpler.
You can't let the suits make technical decisions for you.
If you win the users, everything else will follow. And if you don't, no one will care how comfortingly orthodox your technology choices were.
Power is the ultimate elegance: the Perl program is simpler (has fewer elements), even if the syntax is a bit uglier.

If you want to win in a software business, just take on the hardest problem you can find, use the most powerful language you can get, and wait for your competitors' pointy-haired bosses to revert to the mean.
key_insight: Choosing a more powerful language is a strategic advantage on hard problems, and managerial insistence on “standard” tools often locks competitors into mediocrity.
